The sequence я было uses было as a parenthetical/introductory past form meaning ‘for a moment/at first’ (a marker of an aborted attempt), and и совсем уже решился combines particles and the past reflexive verb решился (решиться) — here meaning both 'decide' and 'dare' (to make up one's mind to act).
segments.p2-1-g766: Но is the adversative conjunction 'but'; кончилось is impersonal/past 'it ended up' (intransitive), and тем is the demonstrative/instrumental pronoun used in the fixed phrase кончилось тем, что — 'it ended up (in) that...' linking to the following clause.
segments.p2-1-g767: Что introduces the content clause 'that'; только here means 'only/merely'; попал is past of попасть and here means 'ended up/was placed' (i.e., came to be) and forms the idiom попал ему под ноги — 'got under his feet' (ему in dative marks 'his' as the possessor/affected person), with под + accusative ноги = 'under (his) feet.'
segments.p2-1-g768: Потому что is the causal conjunction 'because'; в самое последнее мгновение is a temporal phrase with в + accusative ('at the very last instant') where самое intensifies 'последнее' ('the very last'), and мгновение is 'instant/moment.'
segments.p2-1-g769: На ... расстоянии is the standard construction 'at a distance of ...'; двухвершковом is an adjective in the prepositional case modifying расстоянии and names a tiny old Russian measure (two vershoks), and каком-нибудь is an indefinite modifier ('some/somewhat') softening the phrase — 'at some two‑vershok distance.'
segments.p2-1-g770: Не хватило is an impersonal past construction meaning 'there was not enough' or 'I lacked' (often construed with a dative or genitive); духу is the genitive of дух used idiomatically to mean 'courage/nerve' — together: 'I didn't have the nerve.'
segments.p2-1-g771: Он is the subject 'he'; преспокойно is an adverb meaning 'completely calmly/nonchalantly'; прошел is the past of пройти 'passed by/walked by,' and по мне (по + dative) here means 'past me / over me' — idiomatically indicating he went by without noticing or as if over the speaker.
segments.p2-1-g772: И я is simply the coordinated subject 'and I,' linking the second clause to the first and marking the speaker's reaction.
segments.p2-1-g773: Как is the comparative particle 'like/as'; мячик is the diminutive noun 'little ball,' used metaphorically to compare the speaker's movement to a ball being bounced away.
segments.p2-1-g774: Отлетел is the past perfective verb 'flew off'/'was flung off,' indicating a sudden motion away; в сторону is the directional phrase 'to the side' (в + accusative indicating direction).
segments.p2-1-g775: В эту ночь is the temporal phrase 'that night' (в + accusative for a time expression). Я был is the past-tense copula 'I was'; опять bolen = 'again sick' (болен is the short-form adjective used predicatively); в лихорадке is the prepositional phrase 'with a fever' describing the ailment; и бредил is coordinate past tense 'and raved/was delirious' (бредил indicates delirium/raving).
segments.p2-1-g744: "Таким образом" is a fixed adverbial phrase meaning "thus / in this way";grammatically it is formed from the instrumental of "такой" (таким) plus "образом" and functions as a sentence adverb modifying the whole clause that follows.
segments.p2-1-g745: "всё было наконец готово" is an impersonal/categorical statement: "всё" (everything) is the subject, "было" is the past tense of "быть" and "готово" is the short neuter predicate adjective meaning "ready"; "наконец" is an adverb "finally" placed between verb and predicate for emphasis.
segments.p2-1-g746: This is a nominal clause with metaphorical verb use: "красивый бобрик" (adjective + noun in nominative) is the new occupant; "воцарился" is a perfective verb literally "to take the throne / to reign" used figuratively here; "на месте" (на + prepositional) marks location "in/at the place of" and "паскудного енота" is a genitive attribute "of the filthy raccoon."
segments.p2-1-g747: Coordinating conjunction "и" links clauses; "я" is the subject, "начал" is past tense masculine of "начать" (to begin) and takes the infinitive; "помаленьку" is an adverb "little by little" modifying the verb; "приступать" is the imperfective infinitive "to set about / begin (doing)" and "к делу" (к + dative) is the standard collocation meaning "to the business / task."
segments.p2-1-g748: "Нельзя" is an impersonal modal meaning "one cannot / it is not permissible/possible"; the particle "же" adds emphasis; "было" is the past tense auxiliary used here to frame the modality in the past ("it was not possible"); "решиться" is the reflexive infinitive "to decide (to do something)"; "с первого разу" is an adverbial phrase "on the first try" ("с" = from, "первого разу" = first time/occasion).
segments.p2-1-g749: "зря" is a short adverb meaning "in vain / pointlessly" and here stands alone as a parenthetical comment reinforcing that deciding at the first try would be useless.
segments.p2-1-g750: "надо было" is another impersonal past-modal construction meaning "it was necessary"; "это дело" (accusative) is the direct object "this matter/affair" placed before the verb for topicality; "обделать" is a perfective verb meaning "to carry out / do thoroughly"; "умеючи" is an adverb (somewhat colloquial/archaic) meaning "skillfully."
segments.p2-1-g751: "именно" is an intensifying/clarifying adverb "namely / precisely" and it modifies the following adverb "помаленьку" which repeats the idea "little by little" — i.e., do it gradually and deliberately.
segments.p2-1-g752: "Но признаюсь" is a brief parenthetical: "But I confess" or "I must admit". The verb "признаюсь" is present tense first person, used here as a spoken narrative remark.
segments.p2-1-g753: "что" introduces the subordinate content clause. "после многократных попыток" is a temporal prepositional phrase "after repeated attempts." "я даже было начал отчаиваться": "я" subject, "даже" "even," and the particle "было" here is a relic that softens/limits the past action (meaning roughly "for a moment / as if"), combined with "начал" (began) + infinitive "отчаиваться" (to despair) to mean "I even began to despair (but didn’t follow through)."
segments.p2-1-g754: "не состукиваемся никак" is colloquial: "не" negates the verb "состукиваемся" (1st person plural present or generic) meaning "(we) do not / cannot signal by knocking in any way"; "никак" strengthens the negation. The final interjection "да и только!" is a colloquial exclamatory tag meaning roughly "and that's all! / nothing more to say," giving a frustrated closure.
segments.p2-1-g755: "Уж я ль приготовлялся" is a rhetorical/questioning phrase: "Уж" (indeed/already) adds force, "я ль" (я + particle ль) makes it a rhetorical question "wasn't I...?," and "приготовлялся" is the past imperfective "was preparing / getting ready."
segments.p2-1-g756: Parallel rhetorical clause "я ль не намеревался": same particle construction with "не" (not) and "намеревался" (past imperfective "intended / meant to"). Together these two clauses emphasize the speaker's expectation that he was indeed ready and intending to act.
segments.p2-1-g757: "- кажется," is a parenthetical present-tense comment "it seems / it appeared" used to qualify the following imminent action; it functions as a conversational insertion.
segments.p2-1-g758: "вот-вот сейчас" is an emphatic temporal expression "any moment now" (repetition of "вот" intensifies immediacy); "состукнемся" is the perfective future 1st-person-plural "we will/are about to knock (signal)" describing an imminent expected contact.
segments.p2-1-g759: "смотрю" is present-tense narrative "I look / I see" (used like "I notice"); after the dash the result is reported: "и опять я уступил дорогу" — "and again I yielded the way" where "уступил" is past tense (I yielded) and "дорогу" is the direct object in accusative in the idiom "уступить дорогу" = "give way / step aside."
segments.p2-1-g760: "а он и прошел" contrasts the narrator's yielding with the other's behavior: "а" = but/while, "он" = he, and the particle "и" here is a colloquial intensifier; "прошел" is past tense "passed by / went on."
segments.p2-1-g761: "не заметив меня" is a perfective gerund (деепричастие) phrase: "не заметив" = "having not noticed / without noticing," and "меня" is the accusative object "me." It explains the manner in which he passed: he passed without noticing me.
segments.p2-1-g470: На обыкновенном языке is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in ordinary language' (на + prepositional обыкновенном языке). О + noun phrase (о "пункте чести") introduces the theme 'about the
segments.p2-1-g471: "Я вполне был уверен" is past tense with the copula: "I was entirely/quite sure." The parenthetical "чутье-то действительности" is an appositive: чутье (instinct, intuition) with the clitic "-то" adding emphasis ("that very instinct"), and действительности is a genitive specifying "of reality."
segments.p2-1-g472: "несмотря на" is the prepositional phrase "despite," taking the accusative "весь романтизм" ("all the romanticism"). This whole parenthesis concedes that even with romantic sentiment, his instinct for reality remained.
segments.p2-1-g473: This segment is only a punctuation mark (comma) connecting clauses; no words to analyze.
segments.p2-1-g474: "что" introduces the content clause. "все они просто лопнут со смеха" uses future 3rd-person plural "лопнут" (figurative "burst/explode") to mean "they will burst out laughing," with the preposition "со" + noun in genitive "смеха" = "from laughter."
segments.p2-1-g475: "а" introduces a contrast/shift: "and/but the officer..." "не просто" means "not simply/merely," preparing the clarification that follows.
segments.p2-1-g476: "то есть" again gives an explanation: "that is," and "не безобидно" qualifies the preceding phrase as "not harmlessly/inoffensively" (безобидно as adverb).
segments.p2-1-g477: "прибьет меня" is 3rd person singular future perfective from прибить and here colloquially means "will knock/beat me (to a finish)." The pronoun "меня" is the direct object.
segments.p2-1-g478: Again a contrastive "а" ("but/and"), followed by adverb "непременно" ("certainly/without fail"); "коленком" is instrumental indicating the means "with the knee," and "напинает" is a colloquial verb meaning "will kick (me)" (3rd person future/predictive). Word order "коленком меня" places instrument before object for emphasis.
segments.p2-1-g479: "обведя" is a de‑participial (деепричастие) perfective form meaning "having led/while leading/escorting" and modifies the main verb, indicating that the kicks will be administered while he shepherds the speaker around. "таким манером" = "in such a manner/style," and "вокруг биллиарда" = "around the billiard (table)" (вокруг + genitive).
segments.p2-1-g480: The sequence "и потом уж разве смилуется и в окно спустит" uses rhetorical colouring: "и потом уж" = "and then, by the way/indeed," "разве смилуется" is a question-like particle + verb meaning "will he (perhaps) deign to pity," and "и ... спустит" = "and will lower/let (me) down out the window." Both смилуется and спустит are predictive forms.
segments.p2-1-g481: Разумеется is a parenthetical adverb meaning 'of course' or 'naturally' that opens the sentence as a discourse marker; it does not affect the sentence's case or verb forms.
segments.p2-1-g482: This clause has the subject (эта мизерная история) followed by an instrumentally framed limiting phrase (только этим одним со мной) and then the compound predicate with the past-tense ability verb (не могла) + infinitive (окончиться). The instrumental этим одним + со мной means 'by/with just that one thing happening to me'; не могла окончиться is 'could not end' with могла as the past tense of мочь agreeing with the feminine subject.

sentences.p2-1-s1: When, out of the darkness of delusion, by a fervent word of persuasion I drew out a fallen soul, and, utterly full of deep torment, you cursed, wringing your hands, the vice that had ensnared you; When, punishing the forgetful conscience with recollection, you related to me the whole story of what had happened before me, and suddenly, having covered your face with your hands, full of shame and horror, you gave way to tears, indignant, shaken.
sentences.p2-1-s12: Nekrasov. At that time I was only twenty-four years old.
sentences.p2-1-s13: My life was already even then sullen, disorderly, and so lonely as to be almost feral.
sentences.p2-1-s14: I associated with no one and even avoided speaking, and more and more I shut myself away in my corner.
sentences.p2-1-s27: As for me, I would have been perfectly reconciled in my mind.
sentences.p2-1-s28: Even so much that I would have agreed even to a mean expression, with the sole proviso that my face should at the same time be considered terribly clever.
sentences.p2-1-s29: Of course I hated all our clerks, from the first to the last, and despised them all, and at the same time as if I was afraid of them.
sentences.p2-1-s30: There were times when I would even suddenly put them above myself.
sentences.p2-1-s43: Every decent person of our time is, and ought to be, a coward and a slave.
sentences.p2-1-s45: I am deeply convinced of this.
sentences.p2-1-s46: He is made that way and is arranged for that purpose.
sentences.p2-1-s23: I, for example, hated my face, thought it was vile, and even suspected that there was some base expression in it; therefore, every time I went on duty I painfully tried to keep myself as independent as possible so that they would not suspect me of meanness, and with my face to express as much nobility as possible.
sentences.p2-1-s24: "Let it at least be an ugly face," I thought, "but let it, at least, be noble, expressive, and, above all, extraordinarily intelligent."
sentences.p2-1-s25: But I, probably and even painfully, knew that I could never express all those perfections with my face.
sentences.p2-1-s26: But worst of all, I found it positively stupid.
sentences.p2-1-s47: And not now because of some random circumstances, but rather in general, at all times a decent person must be a coward and a slave.
sentences.p2-1-s48: That is the law of nature for all decent people on earth.
sentences.p2-1-s49: Even if one of them should happen to grow bolder about something, let him not take comfort in it and not get carried away: he'll still go mad in front of another.
sentences.p2-1-s50: Such is the sole and everlasting way out.
sentences.p2-1-s35: This tormented me to the point of madness.
sentences.p2-1-s36: I was even afraid, to the point of illness, of being ridiculous, and so I slavishly adored routine in everything that concerned the outward; I would lovingly fall into the common rut and, with my whole soul, be terrified of any eccentricity in myself.
sentences.p2-1-s37: But how could I possibly endure it?
sentences.p2-1-s38: I was morbidly developed, as a person of our time ought to be.
sentences.p2-1-s39: They all, however, were dull and resembled one another like rams in a flock.
sentences.p2-1-s40: Perhaps; only to me alone in the whole office it constantly seemed that I was a coward and a slave; precisely for that reason it seemed that I was developed.
sentences.p2-1-s41: But it not only seemed so, it was actually so in reality: I was a coward and a slave.
sentences.p2-1-s42: I say this without any embarrassment.
sentences.p2-1-s63: Once I had even completely befriended them, I began to visit their homes, play preferans, drink vodka, and expound on production.
sentences.p2-1-s66: But here, allow me to make one digression.
sentences.p2-1-s67: We Russians, generally speaking, have never had those foolish, over-the-top German — and especially French — romantics on whom nothing has any effect: even if the earth should crack under them, even if all France perishes on the barricades, — they are all the same, they will not change even for appearances, and everyone will keep singing their transcendent songs, so to speak, to the coffin of their lives, because they are fools.
sentences.p2-1-s68: As for us, in the Russian land there are no fools; this is well known; that is precisely how we differ from the other German lands.
sentences.p2-1-s31: At that time it somehow happened to me: sometimes I despised it, sometimes I put it above myself.
sentences.p2-1-s32: A developed and decent person cannot be vain without an unlimited demandingness toward himself and without, at certain moments, despising himself to the point of hatred.
sentences.p2-1-s33: But, whether despising or whether placing (it) higher, I almost lowered my eyes before every passerby.
sentences.p2-1-s34: I even carried out experiments: would I endure a look like that on myself, and I always lowered my eyes first.
sentences.p2-1-s19: The other man's ceremonial coat was so worn that it already smelled bad around him.
sentences.p2-1-s20: And yet not one of those gentlemen was embarrassed — neither on account of his clothes, nor on account of his face, nor in any moral way.
sentences.p2-1-s21: Neither the one nor the other imagined that people were looking at them with disgust; and even if they had imagined it, they would have been indifferent — only so long as the superiors did not deign to look.
sentences.p2-1-s22: Now it is perfectly clear to me that I myself, owing to my boundless vanity and therefore also to my exactingness toward myself, used to look at myself very often with a furious dissatisfaction that reached disgust, and consequently, in my mind, attributed my own look to everyone.
sentences.p2-1-s15: At my post in the office I even tried not to look at anyone, and I noticed very clearly that my colleagues not only considered me an eccentric, but — and this, it seemed to me, was especially so — as if they looked at me with some kind of disgust.
sentences.p2-1-s16: It occurred to me: why is it that to no one except me does it seem that they look at him with disgust?
sentences.p2-1-s17: One of our clerks had a disgusting, mottled face, and it even seemed somewhat brigand-like.
sentences.p2-1-s18: I, it seems, wouldn't even have dared to glance at anyone with such an indecent face.
sentences.p2-1-s51: Only asses and their bastards pretend to be brave, but even they only up to a certain wall.
sentences.p2-1-s52: You shouldn't even pay them any attention, because they really signify nothing.
sentences.p2-1-s53: Another circumstance tormented me then: namely, that no one resembled me, and I resembled no one.
sentences.p2-1-s54: "I alone, and they all," I thought — and I pondered.
sentences.p2-1-s55: From this it is clear that I was still quite a boy.
sentences.p2-1-s57: After all, sometimes it became so disgusting to go to the office: it came to the point that I many times returned from duty sick.
sentences.p2-1-s58: But suddenly, for no reason at all, a streak of scepticism and indifference comes on (for me everything came in streaks), and then I myself laugh at my own intolerance and squeamishness, I reproach myself for my romanticism.
sentences.p2-1-s69: Consequently, even transcendent natures are not to be found among us in their pure state.
sentences.p2-1-s70: All these 'positive' contemporary publicists and critics of ours — hunting then after the Kostanzhogls and after uncles like Pyotr Ivanovich, and, foolishly, having taken them for our ideal — made up things about our romantics, considering them as equally transcendent as in Germany or in France.
sentences.p2-1-s71: On the contrary, the qualities of our romantic are completely and directly opposed to the lofty European type, and not a single European yardstick fits here.
sentences.p2-1-s72: (Do let me use this word: "romantic" — a little old‑fashioned word, respectable, well‑deserved and familiar to everyone).
sentences.p2-1-s59: Sometimes I don't want to speak to anyone, and at other times I get to the point that not only will I begin to talk, but I'll even think of getting together with them as friends.
sentences.p2-1-s60: All my squeamishness would at once, out of nowhere, disappear.
sentences.p2-1-s61: Who knows, maybe I never had it at all, but it was put-on, taken from books?
sentences.p2-1-s62: I still haven't resolved this question.
